summary:
The Han Salt and Iron Monopoly (established c. 119 BC under Emperor Wu) was a state takeover of China's two most essential and profitable commodities, implemented to fund the costly wars against the Xiongnu without raising direct taxes on the peasantry. State-appointed officials managed production and distribution, effectively displacing the powerful merchant class that had previously controlled these industries. The revenues were substantial — salt and iron were necessities consumed by every household.

The policy generated fierce intellectual debate, culminating in the famous Salt and Iron Debates (81 BC) convened after Emperor Wu's death, in which Confucian scholars argued against state commercial involvement on moral grounds (merchants corrupt society; the state should not compete with the people) while government officials defended the fiscal and strategic necessity. The resulting text, the Yantielun (Discourses on Salt and Iron), is one of the earliest records of systematic economic and political debate in world history. The monopoly system, with modifications, persisted through successive Chinese dynasties for over two thousand years, shaping China's political economy and the subordinate status of merchants relative to officials.
